3: Although countries adhering to the United Nations Conference on Climate Change (COP21) agreed to keep the increase in the global temperature below 2 °C, little policy attention is given to the livestock sector as reduction target (Bailey et al., 2014), even though animal-based production, particularly the beef supply chain, is charged as a major contributor to green-house gas (GHG) emissions and deforestation (Ruviaro et al., 2016). However, environmental impacts per kg of edible beef (De Vries et al., 2015) vary largely (from 8.6 to 35.2 kg CO2 equivalents for GHG emissions and from 12.1 to 47.2 m² for land use). This variability is probably due to differences in methodological choices, but also to differences in beef production systems (e.g. origin of the calves, extensive or intensive conditions, duration of fattening phase, diet formulation). In several western countries, including Italy, systems based on the confinement of specialized breeds are combined with beef grazing systems using both local and specialized breeds in a mixed pro- duction scenario. Specialized breeds usually have the potential for reducing the amount of inputs (i.e. feed), losses of nutrients and unwanted outputs (e.g. methane and nitrogen compounds) to produce a certain amount of meat as compared with local breeds. Conversely, local breeds may be better suited to use low quality roughage. Therefore, different systems may imply trade-offs be- tween different forms of impact: some systems may sustain biodiversity and promote carbon sequestration, others may be more efficient in terms of feed conversion ratios (Galka, 2004); therefore, from an environmental perspective, the identification of beef production best practices is not simple. For instance, pasture- based systems may have lower environmental impacts due to the low inputs needed in the production process, although requiring more land. In addition, in these systems, the implementation of management strategies, including supplemental feeding, may generate many benefits such as increased animal productivity and reduced emissions per beef unit without the need for new livestock areas (Ruviaro et al., 2016). Erb et al. (2016) stated that if crop production efficiency will increase significantly, even a worldwide adoption of dietary levels similar to those currently used in the western countries may be sustainable. The same authors showed that even a marked intensification of livestock production systems wouldn't be as effective in reducing deforestation. In addition, the information concerning the reduced impact on land use and a higher degree of sustainability, may be used as a marketing strategy in order to differentiate the product and meet consumers' ethical needs for food (Ruviaro et al., 2014; Gianezini et al., 2014). The Podolian beef production system is a traditional Italian enterprise conducted for centuries in semi-arid environments with poor vegetation, as indicated by anatomical and physiological ad- aptations such as skin pigmentation, well-developed dewlap and sturdy hooves. These animals are commonly kept on pasture, with either no- or nocturnal shelter, with no or little supplementation for cows and calves, whereas young males may receive concen- trates in the finishing period. This breed occupies less favored areas characterized by upland, erosive, low fertility and not irrigated soils, therefore unsuitable for cost-effective crop production. Land can be used for several different purposes and if land use options are included in the assessment, results on environmental

performances may change (Thomassen et al., 2008). Wiedemann et al. (2015) observed that grass fed beef cattle occupied more land than grain fed animals, but most of it was non-arable land. Conversely, when only cropland occupation was considered, opposite results were found with grain fed beef using more land.

Thus, this study aimed to compare the sustainability of four different beef production systems using the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach. These systems are the most common systems relying on bull production in Italy (veal production was excluded as the final product is quite different and oriented towards different market segments) and include local and specialized breeds initially kept on pasture and then finished in confinement, imported specialized breeds finished in confinement, and specialized breeds permanently kept in confinement.

5: Life Cycle Assessment is the assessment of inputs, outputs and environmental impacts of any production systems through pro- duction, usage, and disposal. According to the ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a, b), the principles and framework for LCA include four distinct phases: 1) definition of the goal and scope (including functional unit, and limits of the system); 2) the life cycle inventory (LCI) analysis (including in and output data collection for all processes); 3) the life cycle impact assessment (LCIA); 4) the life cycle interpretation.
6: The goal of this study was to compare four beef-production systems in terms of global warming potential (GWP, kg CO2 -eq), acidification potential (AP, kg SO2 -eq), eutrophication potential (EP, g NO3- water depletion (WD, m³) and land occupation (LO, m2/ year). The functional unit was 1 kg of live weight of marketed beef cattle.
7: The system boundaries of the different beef-production systems concerned a cradle-to-farm gate analysis (Fig. 1). We took into ac- count any on-farm activities (i.e. feed production, electricity, and energy use, manure and animal management) as primary data, and the corresponding emissions. The consumption of energy and the emissions from activities conducted off-farm were retrieved from Ecoinvent3 allocation default - system (building materials, tap water, transports, distiller's dried grains, field bean, and electricity low-medium voltage), USLCI (fossil fuels, electricity, and natural gases), and Agri-footprint - mass allocation (fertilizers, fodder, transports, and bedding materials) databases. The transport of off- farm feeds and bedding material along with the transport associ- ated with the transfer of imported calves were also included in the assessment.

A short description of these 4 farming systems is given below.

2.1.3.1. Podolian system. Podolian cattle are a native breed, tradi- tionally kept on pasture in Southern Italy, with nocturnal shelter. Often this system is based on low fertility soils (organic matter 1.24%, P2O5 24 mg/kg, K2O 381 mg/kg) and low production per- manent pastures with herbage production characterized by uneven distribution throughout the year (Braghieri et al., 2011). Biomass availability in dry matter (DM), in fact, is much lower in summer (568.03 + 399.74 g/m²) and has higher DM percentage (66.72 + 6.12%) compared with spring season (849.72 + 399.74 g/ m² and 29.98 + 6.12%, for herbage mass and DM, respectively). In addition, these pastures are characterized mainly by Gramineae (70%), Composites (15%) and less by Legumes (6%).

Podolian cows are seasonal and mate in spring, between February and April (Napolitano et al., 2005). They graze natural pasture throughout the year, receiving supplementation (meadow hay and straw) during summer. Calves are dam reared, suckling and feeding on pasture for 7-10 months. At this age, they are moved to a straw bedded barn provided with an outdoor paddock where they are fattened until slaughtering age (16 mo. on average).

2.1.3.2. Specialized extensive (SE). This beef system is similar to the previous with a cow-calf period on pasture (analogous to that previously described for PoS in terms of productivity and chemical and botanical composition) and a subsequent fattening phase in confinement until slaughtering at about 16 months of age. How- ever, this system relies on specialized beef breeds (e.g. Limousine, Charolaise, Chianina, Romagnola, Marchigiana).

2.1.3.3. Fattening system (FS). This system involves the intensive fattening of beef cattle ("broutard") mainly imported at 9-12 months of age from France and Eastern European countries where the animals have been kept with a cow-calf phase on pasture (Table 2a) based on permanent grassland (biomass availability of 350-650 g/m² richer in Gramineae than in Legumes and

Composites (Baumont et al., 2012), and a subsequent sub-fattening period (Table 2b) (Nguyen et al., 2012). The animals are reared in specialized farms provided with barn with concrete or slatted floor until slaughtering at 600-700 kg of live weight.

2.1.3.4. Cow-calf intensive (CCI). This kind of beef production in- volves conducting cow-calf operations, with specialized beef ani- mals constantly kept in confinement and a following fattening period in intensive conditions until a slaughter weight of 650-700 kg.
For FS system data from cow-calf phase and a subsequent pre- fattening period abroad were retrieved from data reported by Nguyen et al. (2012).

Inventory data covering livestock production, crop cultivations, straw for litter (only for farms without grazing system, kg), inputs of purchased feed, electricity, and farm extension (divided in arable and pasture, ha/year) were primary data. A daily pasture intake of about 13 kg of DM in PoS and SE cow with calf was assumed (Bovolenta et al., 2002). The feed consumption in the intensive systems (CCI and FS) and in the fattening phase of PoS and SE was retrieved by interviews to the farmers (Table 2a and 2b).

The "on farm" feed was assumed to be transported for 1 km by a tractor; the bought feed transport by truck (Transport, freight lorry>32 metric ton, EURO3, RER), has been computed based on the distance from the farm: for the maize grain, we assumed a road travel of 100 km for the farms located in northern Italy, and 900 km for the farms in Basilicata region. Based on similar criterion, we assumed the distances for the others national feeds. According with Nguyen et al. (2010), a 10000 km travel by ship for soybean from South America and a 12000 km travel for palm oil from Malaysia and maize from North America were assessed; moreover, a travel with truck from Italian harbor to farm gate was added to this computation. For the transfer of the animals from France to Italy we considered a Transport, freight lorry>32 metric ton, EURO3 (RER) truck. For diesel provision, we considered a freight lorry 3.5-7.5 metric ton, EURO3 (RER) truck.
Diesel consumption was included in each feed input selected in SimaPro database.

confinement (SE); 6 farms conducting high grain fattening of specialized beef breed imported calves (FS); 5 farms conducting cow-calf operations, with specialized beef animals constantly kept in confinement (CCI); 7 farms conducting cow-calf operations, with Podolian cattle maintained on pasture in the first phase and finished in confinement (PoS). A short description of these 4 farming systems is given below.

2.1.3.1. Podolian system. Podolian cattle are a native breed, tradi- tionally kept on pasture in Southern Italy, with nocturnal shelter. Often this system is based on low fertility soils (organic matter 1.24%, P2O5 24 mg/kg, K2O 381 mg/kg) and low production per- manent pastures with herbage production characterized by uneven distribution throughout the year (Braghieri et al., 2011). Biomass availability in dry matter (DM), in fact, is much lower in summer (568.03 + 399.74 g/m²) and has higher DM percentage (66.72 + 6.12%) compared with spring season (849.72 + 399.74 g/ m² and 29.98 + 6.12%, for herbage mass and DM, respectively). In addition, these pastures are characterized mainly by Gramineae (70%), Composites (15%) and less by Legumes (6%).

Podolian cows are seasonal and mate in spring, between February and April (Napolitano et al., 2005). They graze natural pasture throughout the year, receiving supplementation (meadow hay and straw) during summer. Calves are dam reared, suckling and feeding on pasture for 7-10 months. At this age, they are moved to a straw bedded barn provided with an outdoor paddock where they are fattened until slaughtering age (16 mo. on average).

2.1.3.2. Specialized extensive (SE). This beef system is similar to the previous with a cow-calf period on pasture (analogous to that previously described for PoS in terms of productivity and chemical and botanical composition) and a subsequent fattening phase in confinement until slaughtering at about 16 months of age. How- ever, this system relies on specialized beef breeds (e.g. Limousine, Charolaise, Chianina, Romagnola, Marchigiana).

2.1.3.3. Fattening system (FS). This system involves the intensive fattening of beef cattle ("broutard") mainly imported at 9-12 months of age from France and Eastern European countries where the animals have been kept with a cow-calf phase on pasture (Table 2a) based on permanent grassland (biomass availability of 350-650 g/m² richer in Gramineae than in Legumes and

Composites (Baumont et al., 2012), and a subsequent sub-fattening period (Table 2b) (Nguyen et al., 2012). The animals are reared in specialized farms provided with barn with concrete or slatted floor until slaughtering at 600-700 kg of live weight.

2.1.3.4. Cow-calf intensive (CCI). This kind of beef production in- volves conducting cow-calf operations, with specialized beef ani- mals constantly kept in confinement and a following fattening period in intensive conditions until a slaughter weight of 650-700 kg.
For FS system data from cow-calf phase and a subsequent pre- fattening period abroad were retrieved from data reported by Nguyen et al. (2012).

Inventory data covering livestock production, crop cultivations, straw for litter (only for farms without grazing system, kg), inputs of purchased feed, electricity, and farm extension (divided in arable and pasture, ha/year) were primary data. A daily pasture intake of about 13 kg of DM in PoS and SE cow with calf was assumed (Bovolenta et al., 2002). The feed consumption in the intensive systems (CCI and FS) and in the fattening phase of PoS and SE was retrieved by interviews to the farmers (Table 2a and 2b).

The "on farm" feed was assumed to be transported for 1 km by a tractor; the bought feed transport by truck (Transport, freight lorry>32 metric ton, EURO3, RER), has been computed based on the distance from the farm: for the maize grain, we assumed a road travel of 100 km for the farms located in northern Italy, and 900 km for the farms in Basilicata region. Based on similar criterion, we assumed the distances for the others national feeds. According with Nguyen et al. (2010), a 10000 km travel by ship for soybean from South America and a 12000 km travel for palm oil from Malaysia and maize from North America were assessed; moreover, a travel with truck from Italian harbor to farm gate was added to this computation. For the transfer of the animals from France to Italy we considered a Transport, freight lorry>32 metric ton, EURO3 (RER) truck. For diesel provision, we considered a freight lorry 3.5-7.5 metric ton, EURO3 (RER) truck.
Diesel consumption was included in each feed input selected in SimaPro database.

5: To compare the cradle-to-farm gate, global warming potential (GWP, kg CO2 -eq), acidification potential (AP, g SO2 -eq),

eutrophication potential (EP, g NO3 -eq), water depletion (WD, m3), and land occupation (LO, m2) LCA methodology (SimaPro 8.01 PhD, PRé Consultants, 2013) was used. We computed the GWP, an index for estimating the climate change contribution due to the atmo- spheric emission of GHGs, according to the CO2-equivalent factors defined by the IPCC (2007): CO2, 1; CH4, 25; and N20, 298. These factors were set based on a time horizon of 100 years. To calculate the AP of the different trace gases, we used the SO2-equivalent factors for SO2 and SOx = 1, NO2 and NOx = 0.7, and NH3 = 1.88 (Heijungs et al., 1992). To calculate EP of the different pollutants, we used the POx-equivalent factors for NO2 and NOx = 0.13, NH3 = 0.33, NO3 = 0.1, and P = 3.06 (Heijungs et al., 1992). The ReCiPe Midpoint (I) module of SimaPro package was used in the evaluation of GWP, AP, WD, and LO, whereas EP was assessed by the Edip LCA Food method. This method was chosen because it includes both fresh and marine water. In addition, results are expressed in terms of NO3 -eq as in the majority of the literature.

7: According to Wiedemann et al. (2015), human edible protein conversion efficiency (HEPCE) was determined by dividing the protein content of the meat yield weight by human edible protein in feed consumed by the animals.
8: We calculated GWP, AP, EP, WD, and LO of each farm using the LCA model. Then these data were analyzed by one-way (general linear model procedure) ANOVA, using the production system as factor. Data were analyzed using SAS software (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC). The same analysis was performed on the data related to the HEPCE index. Standard errors were calculated as the ratio be- tween standard deviation and the squared root of the sample size.
9: Table 4 shows the cradle to-farm gate life cycle GWP, land occupation, water depletion, acidification and euthrophying emis- sions related to four different beef production systems. In agree- ment with previous studies, which stated that intensifying animal production is advocated to mitigate certain environmental impacts, such as emission of greenhouse gases (e.g. Pelletier et al., 2010), in our experiment, intensive systems, such as FS and CCI showed lower GWP values than systems partially based on pasture such as PoS, and even SE (P < 0.01).

These results can be attributed to the higher animal perfor- mances obtained with intensive production systems coupled with reduced ruminant methane emissions produced by concentrate based diets. In fact, both in PoS and SE systems, the main contrib- utors to high GWP (Fig. 2) are enteric emissions (85% and 75% in PoS and SE, respectively), due to the larger use of forages than con- centrates. Similar results about the fundamental role played by

enteric fermentation on GWP were obtained by other authors (Dick et al., 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2015). On the contrary, in group FS the inputs needed during the pre-fattening phase abroad (including transport to Italy and feeding supplementation of calves) and the feeding inputs in the fattening period (Fig. 2) were the higher contributors to GWP (57% and 31%, respectively) while enteric emissions had a lower impact (10%). In the CCI system GWP was mainly due to enteric emissions (48%) and feed input during the fattening phase (34%). Manure emission contribution of CCI (5%) was more marked than in the other systems (Fig. 2) due to the fact that all the production phases in this system are conducted in confinement. Livestock manure management accounts for almost 10% of GHG emissions from agriculture globally (Owen and Silver, 2015). This contribution, in terms of N20 and CH4, varies in rela- tion to on farm conditions and management decisions, such as temperature and water availability, storage period and manure spreading to land (Chadwick et al., 2011).

Acidification potential (Table 4) was significantly affected by production system < 0.001). The highest values were observed in CCI (0.30 + 0.02 kg SO2 -eq) compared with the other three beef production systems. These results are in agreement with Castanheira et al. (2010), who found that the largest source of emissions to air and water from cattle production systems is the production of concentrates. Other authors (Ogino et al., 2016) found higher AP for intensive systems due to the purchased feed and the higher NH3 emissions from manure (housing and manure storage). In dairy cattle, the main contributors to terrestrial acidification are emissions of ammonia from manure management and manure application for the cultivation of feed raw materials (Broekema and Kramer, 2014). In the CCI system AP was mainly (42%) affected by manure emissions (Fig. 2) and by the feeding input during pre- fattening and fattening phases (26% and 27%, respectively).

As for eutrophication potential, FS and PoS tended (P < 0.10) to show lower values compared with CCI and SE systems (Table 4), with higher contribution (Fig. 2) of feeding input during the pre- fattening phase (44%). According with Nguyen et al. (2010), ni- trate leaching from agricultural soils is the most important contributor among the group of substances responsible for eutro- phication. In agreement with the same authors, in FS the major contributor to EP (Fig. 2) was the emission from feeding inputs during the fattening phase (48%). Dick et al. (2015) found higher value of EP in intensive systems compared with the extensive. However, according to the same authors and also to De Vries et al. (2015), the values obtained both for AP and EP are dependent on the local soil and climatic conditions and makes difficult compar- ison between studies.

As for land occupation (Table 4), significant differences were observed between the four systems (P < 0.001). Similarly, in Brazil Dick et al. (2015) observed that extensive systems occupied more land than intensive systems. Farm extension is the main contrib- utor of LO in PoS, SE and CCI systems (64%, 62% and 38%, respec- tively), while in the system FS, LO is mainly influenced by feeding inputs during the pre-fattening phase abroad (Fig. 2). Feeding inputs in the pre-fattering phase

Fig. 2. Contribution of processes to global warming potential (GWP), land occupation (LO), acidification (AP) and euthropying (EP) potentials in the four beef production systems; CCI: Cow-Calf Intensive; FS: fattening system; PoS: Podolian System; SE: Specialized extensive.

occupation states for the area of land that is being in use and thus temporarily unavailable for other purposes (Nguyen et al., 2010). Land use and land use change (e.g. from forest to cropland) can add or remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, thereby contrib- uting to an increase or decrease in GWP. LO per unit of product is lower for most concentrate-based systems compared with roughage-based systems (De Vries et al., 2015), mainly owing to the higher growth rate of calves in concentrate-based systems. How- ever, land use impact evaluation should also take into account the quality of the lands used as pasture that in the PoS and SE are marginal (upland and of low fertility). With no realistic alternative to extensive farming, these lands would risk desertification. In addition, since the land is used extensively, it may still serve mul- tiple beneficial uses, including recreation and energy production through wind farming and solar harvesting (Turney and Fthenakis, 2011). Conversely, in intensive systems feeding is mainly based on cereals, products that humans could consume directly. Thus, the degree of competition with human nutrition should be another aspect to be considered in terms of sustainable production as much of the energy value is lost during conversion from plant to animal matter (Gerbens-Leenes and Nonhebel, 2005).

As regards water depletion (Table 4), no significant effect of production system was observed, although PoS showed lower values compared with the others systems. According to Gerbens- Leenes et al. (2013), the water depletion of the beef chain produc- tion is dominated by the animal feed, and WD of concentrates is

roughly five times larger than the WD of roughages. However, the values of water consumption vary appreciably among different studies. For instance, Ridoutt et al. (2012) observed 3.3-221 1/kg LWG in Australia, whereas Dick et al. (2015) observed 0.0949-0.217 m³ /kg LWG in southern Brazil.

occupation states for the area of land that is being in use and thus temporarily unavailable for other purposes (Nguyen et al., 2010). LO per unit of product is lower for most concentrate-based systems compared with roughage-based systems (De Vries et al., 2015), mainly owing to the higher growth rate of calves in concentrate-based systems. Feeding inputs in the fattering phase
Feeding inputs in the pre-fattering phase

Fig. 2. Contribution of processes to global warming potential (GWP), land occupation (LO), acidification (AP) and euthropying (EP) potentials in the four beef production systems; CCI: Cow-Calf Intensive; FS: fattening system; PoS: Podolian System; SE: Specialized extensive.

occupation states for the area of land that is being in use and thus temporarily unavailable for other purposes (Nguyen et al., 2010). Land use and land use change (e.g. from forest to cropland) can add or remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, thereby contrib- uting to an increase or decrease in GWP. LO per unit of product is lower for most concentrate-based systems compared with roughage-based systems (De Vries et al., 2015), mainly owing to the higher growth rate of calves in concentrate-based systems. How- ever, land use impact evaluation should also take into account the quality of the lands used as pasture that in the PoS and SE are marginal (upland and of low fertility). With no realistic alternative to extensive farming, these lands would risk desertification. In addition, since the land is used extensively, it may still serve mul- tiple beneficial uses, including recreation and energy production through wind farming and solar harvesting (Turney and Fthenakis, 2011). Conversely, in intensive systems feeding is mainly based on cereals, products that humans could consume directly. Thus, the degree of competition with human nutrition should be another aspect to be considered in terms of sustainable production as much of the energy value is lost during conversion from plant to animal matter (Gerbens-Leenes and Nonhebel, 2005).

As regards water depletion (Table 4), no significant effect of production system was observed, although PoS showed lower values compared with the others systems. According to Gerbens- Leenes et al. (2013), the water depletion of the beef chain produc- tion is dominated by the animal feed, and WD of concentrates is

roughly five times larger than the WD of roughages. However, the values of water consumption vary appreciably among different studies. For instance, Ridoutt et al. (2012) observed 3.3-221 1/kg LWG in Australia, whereas Dick et al. (2015) observed 0.0949-0.217 m³ /kg LWG in southern Brazil.

As for efficiency comparisons, the degree of competition with human nutrition was taken into account as in certain farming systems, feeds are not humanly consumable, and to determine the real efficiency of the system, such feeds were not included in the computation. Although human edible protein conversion efficiency (HEPCE) was not significantly influenced by production system, farms PoS showed a higher HEPCE index (0.44 + 0.08), particularly when compared with CCI system (0.17 + 0.09) but also with sys- tems FS and SE (0.34 + 0.08 and 0.39 + 0.08, respectively). Wiedemann et al. (2015) found that total HEPCE was higher for grass finished boneless beef than for grain finished boneless beef, indicating that the former product yielded more human edible protein using less human edible protein inputs.

Besides beef production, grass-based systems may provide other services, such as provision of clean drinking water, preser- vation and enhancement of biodiversity (Henle et al., 2008), con- servation of cultural landscapes (Plieninger et al., 2006), contribution to the socio-economic viability of many rural areas particularly in marginal areas (De Rancourt et al., 2006), enhance- ment of meat quality and animal welfare levels as perceived by consumers (Sitz et al., 2005). Therefore, for extensive production

systems, especially in marginal areas, the outputs in LCA analysis have to refer not only to material products but also to other non- commodity outputs and non-marketable public goods, named|emissions. Feeding inputs in the pre-fattering phase

Fig. 2. Contribution of processes to global warming potential (GWP), land occupation (LO), acidification (AP) and euthropying (EP) potentials in the four beef production systems; CCI: Cow-Calf Intensive; FS: fattening system; PoS: Podolian System; SE: Specialized extensive.

occupation states for the area of land that is being in use and thus temporarily unavailable for other purposes (Nguyen et al., 2010). Land use and land use change (e.g. from forest to cropland) can add or remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, thereby contrib- uting to an increase or decrease in GWP. LO per unit of product is lower for most concentrate-based systems compared with roughage-based systems (De Vries et al., 2015), mainly owing to the higher growth rate of calves in concentrate-based systems. How- ever, land use impact evaluation should also take into account the quality of the lands used as pasture that in the PoS and SE are marginal (upland and of low fertility). With no realistic alternative to extensive farming, these lands would risk desertification. In addition, since the land is used extensively, it may still serve mul- tiple beneficial uses, including recreation and energy production through wind farming and solar harvesting (Turney and Fthenakis, 2011). Conversely, in intensive systems feeding is mainly based on cereals, products that humans could consume directly. Thus, the degree of competition with human nutrition should be another aspect to be considered in terms of sustainable production as much of the energy value is lost during conversion from plant to animal matter (Gerbens-Leenes and Nonhebel, 2005).

As regards water depletion (Table 4), no significant effect of production system was observed, although PoS showed lower values compared with the others systems. According to Gerbens- Leenes et al. (2013), the water depletion of the beef chain produc- tion is dominated by the animal feed, and WD of concentrates is

roughly five times larger than the WD of roughages. However, the values of water consumption vary appreciably among different studies. For instance, Ridoutt et al. (2012) observed 3.3-221 1/kg LWG in Australia, whereas Dick et al. (2015) observed 0.0949-0.217 m³ /kg LWG in southern Brazil.

As for efficiency comparisons, the degree of competition with human nutrition was taken into account as in certain farming systems, feeds are not humanly consumable, and to determine the real efficiency of the system, such feeds were not included in the computation. Although human edible protein conversion efficiency (HEPCE) was not significantly influenced by production system, farms PoS showed a higher HEPCE index (0.44 + 0.08), particularly when compared with CCI system (0.17 + 0.09) but also with sys- tems FS and SE (0.34 + 0.08 and 0.39 + 0.08, respectively). Wiedemann et al. (2015) found that total HEPCE was higher for grass finished boneless beef than for grain finished boneless beef, indicating that the former product yielded more human edible protein using less human edible protein inputs.

Besides beef production, grass-based systems may provide other services, such as provision of clean drinking water, preser- vation and enhancement of biodiversity (Henle et al., 2008), con- servation of cultural landscapes (Plieninger et al., 2006), contribution to the socio-economic viability of many rural areas particularly in marginal areas (De Rancourt et al., 2006), enhance- ment of meat quality and animal welfare levels as perceived by consumers (Sitz et al., 2005). Therefore, for extensive production

systems, especially in marginal areas, the outputs in LCA analysis have to refer not only to material products but also to other non- commodity outputs and non-marketable public goods, named|emissions. Feeding inputs in the fattering phase
Feeding inputs in the pre-fattering phase

Fig. 2. Contribution of processes to global warming potential (GWP), land occupation (LO), acidification (AP) and euthropying (EP) potentials in the four beef production systems; CCI: Cow-Calf Intensive; FS: fattening system; PoS: Podolian System; SE: Specialized extensive.

occupation states for the area of land that is being in use and thus temporarily unavailable for other purposes (Nguyen et al., 2010). Land use and land use change (e.g. from forest to cropland) can add or remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, thereby contrib- uting to an increase or decrease in GWP. LO per unit of product is lower for most concentrate-based systems compared with roughage-based systems (De Vries et al., 2015), mainly owing to the higher growth rate of calves in concentrate-based systems. How- ever, land use impact evaluation should also take into account the quality of the lands used as pasture that in the PoS and SE are marginal (upland and of low fertility). With no realistic alternative to extensive farming, these lands would risk desertification. In addition, since the land is used extensively, it may still serve mul- tiple beneficial uses, including recreation and energy production through wind farming and solar harvesting (Turney and Fthenakis, 2011). Conversely, in intensive systems feeding is mainly based on cereals, products that humans could consume directly. Thus, the degree of competition with human nutrition should be another aspect to be considered in terms of sustainable production as much of the energy value is lost during conversion from plant to animal matter (Gerbens-Leenes and Nonhebel, 2005).

As regards water depletion (Table 4), no significant effect of production system was observed, although PoS showed lower values compared with the others systems. According to Gerbens- Leenes et al. (2013), the water depletion of the beef chain produc- tion is dominated by the animal feed, and WD of concentrates is

roughly five times larger than the WD of roughages. However, the values of water consumption vary appreciably among different studies. For instance, Ridoutt et al. (2012) observed 3.3-221 1/kg LWG in Australia, whereas Dick et al. (2015) observed 0.0949-0.217 m³ /kg LWG in southern Brazil.

As for efficiency comparisons, the degree of competition with human nutrition was taken into account as in certain farming systems, feeds are not humanly consumable, and to determine the real efficiency of the system, such feeds were not included in the computation. Although human edible protein conversion efficiency (HEPCE) was not significantly influenced by production system, farms PoS showed a higher HEPCE index (0.44 + 0.08), particularly when compared with CCI system (0.17 + 0.09) but also with sys- tems FS and SE (0.34 + 0.08 and 0.39 + 0.08, respectively). Wiedemann et al. (2015) found that total HEPCE was higher for grass finished boneless beef than for grain finished boneless beef, indicating that the former product yielded more human edible protein using less human edible protein inputs.

Besides beef production, grass-based systems may provide other services, such as provision of clean drinking water, preser- vation and enhancement of biodiversity (Henle et al., 2008), con- servation of cultural landscapes (Plieninger et al., 2006), contribution to the socio-economic viability of many rural areas particularly in marginal areas (De Rancourt et al., 2006), enhance- ment of meat quality and animal welfare levels as perceived by consumers (Sitz et al., 2005). Therefore, for extensive production

systems, especially in marginal areas, the outputs in LCA analysis have to refer not only to material products but also to other non- commodity outputs and non-marketable public goods, named|emissions. Feeding inputs in the fattering phase
Feeding inputs in the pre-fattering phase

Fig. 2. Contribution of processes to global warming potential (GWP), land occupation (LO), acidification (AP) and euthropying (EP) potentials in the four beef production systems; CCI: Cow-Calf Intensive; FS: fattening system; PoS: Podolian System; SE: Specialized extensive.

occupation states for the area of land that is being in use and thus temporarily unavailable for other purposes (Nguyen et al., 2010). Land use and land use change (e.g. from forest to cropland) can add or remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, thereby contrib- uting to an increase or decrease in GWP. LO per unit of product is lower for most concentrate-based systems compared with roughage-based systems (De Vries et al., 2015), mainly owing to the higher growth rate of calves in concentrate-based systems. How- ever, land use impact evaluation should also take into account the quality of the lands used as pasture that in the PoS and SE are marginal (upland and of low fertility). With no realistic alternative to extensive farming, these lands would risk desertification. In addition, since the land is used extensively, it may still serve mul- tiple beneficial uses, including recreation and energy production through wind farming and solar harvesting (Turney and Fthenakis, 2011). Conversely, in intensive systems feeding is mainly based on cereals, products that humans could consume directly. Thus, the degree of competition with human nutrition should be another aspect to be considered in terms of sustainable production as much of the energy value is lost during conversion from plant to animal matter (Gerbens-Leenes and Nonhebel, 2005).

As regards water depletion (Table 4), no significant effect of production system was observed, although PoS showed lower values compared with the others systems. According to Gerbens- Leenes et al. (2013), the water depletion of the beef chain produc- tion is dominated by the animal feed, and WD of concentrates is

roughly five times larger than the WD of roughages. However, the values of water consumption vary appreciably among different studies. For instance, Ridoutt et al. (2012) observed 3.3-221 1/kg LWG in Australia, whereas Dick et al. (2015) observed 0.0949-0.217 m³ /kg LWG in southern Brazil.

As for efficiency comparisons, the degree of competition with human nutrition was taken into account as in certain farming systems, feeds are not humanly consumable, and to determine the real efficiency of the system, such feeds were not included in the computation. Although human edible protein conversion efficiency (HEPCE) was not significantly influenced by production system, farms PoS showed a higher HEPCE index (0.44 + 0.08), particularly when compared with CCI system (0.17 + 0.09) but also with sys- tems FS and SE (0.34 + 0.08 and 0.39 + 0.08, respectively). Wiedemann et al. (2015) found that total HEPCE was higher for grass finished boneless beef than for grain finished boneless beef, indicating that the former product yielded more human edible protein using less human edible protein inputs.

Besides beef production, grass-based systems may provide other services, such as provision of clean drinking water, preser- vation and enhancement of biodiversity (Henle et al., 2008), con- servation of cultural landscapes (Plieninger et al., 2006), contribution to the socio-economic viability of many rural areas particularly in marginal areas (De Rancourt et al., 2006), enhance- ment of meat quality and animal welfare levels as perceived by consumers (Sitz et al., 2005). Therefore, for extensive production

systems, especially in marginal areas, the outputs in LCA analysis have to refer not only to material products but also to other non- commodity outputs and non-marketable public goods, named|emissions.
